coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of yellow emissive heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots fluorescent probe with superior quantum yield for fast-track ionic detection in aqueous media
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Patra, Rashmiranjan
Panda, Pradeep Kumar
Hung, Chang-Yuan
Dash, Pranjyan
Yang, Po-Chih
Hsieh, Chien-Te
coredata.dc:description: In this work, heteroatom (B and N) doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) materials were synthesized via facile hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as a nitrogen source and 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (3APBA) as a boron source. The synthesis parameters were optimized and optimal synthesis temperature and reaction time were 260 °C and 2 h, respectively. The chemical structures were characterized using various techniques. The experimental results showed that B,N co-doped CQDs had the strongest fluorescence intensity when the weight ratio of 3APBA:o-PD was 3:1 and showed a phenomenal quantum yield of 59% with high selectivity for Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions, with quenching constants 1.25 × 103 M–1 and 8.3 × 103 M–1, respectively. The high fluorescence quenching was attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer phenomenon, making it suitable for fluorescent sensing materials. Cytotoxicity data revealed synthesized materials are safe for biomedical applications within a 1–10 µg/mL concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Heteroatom doping
Ion sensing
Fluorescence probe
Sustainable environment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic routes of BNCQDs using o-PD and 3APBA as precursors.

Photoluminescence spectral intensity of NCQDs under various (a) reaction temperature (b) reaction time, and (c) comparison of photoluminescence intensities within different doping amounts of BNCQDs, (d), (e) and (f) corresponding digital photographs.
HR-TEM images of (a) NCQDs, (b) NCQDs crystal planes, (c) BNCQD-5, and (d) BNCQD-5 crystal planes.
(a) XPS spectra of NCQDs and BNCQDs, High resolution (b) C1s spectra (c) O1s spectra, (e) N1s spectra of BNCQD-5, and (f) FTIR spectra of NCQDs and BNCQD-5.

PL excitation and emission spectra of (a) NCQDs and (b) BNCQD-5, (c) photos of NCQDs and BNCQDs under irradiation (left: visible light and right: UV light), (d) UV–VIS spectra and Tauc's plot of (e) NCQDs and (f) BNCQDs.
Photoluminescence spectra of (a) NCQDs, (d) BNCQD-5, intensity ratio of (b) NCQDs, (e) BNCQD-5, and color variations of (c) NCQDs, (f) BNCQD-5 in various metal ions.
Fluorescence quenching of NCQDs after adding different concentrations of (a) iron and (c) mercury ions, (b) and (d) are the corresponding fluorescence quenching of BNCQD-5.
UV absorption of (a) NCQDs and (b) BNCQD-5 after adding Fe3+ ions, (c) NCQDs, and (d) BNCQD-5 after adding Hg2+ ions.
(a) The pH value effect on PL spectrum of BNCQD-5 and (b) corresponding digital images under UV light.
Cell viability of NCQDs and BNCQD-5 samples against 3T3 fibroblast cells.
Comparative performance of NCQDs and BNCQD-5 with literature known sensors for Fe3+ ion.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of yellow emissive heteroatom doped carbon quantum dots fluorescent probe with superior quantum yield for fast-track ionic detection in aqueous media

National Taipei University of Technology (Taipei Tech)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, heteroatom (B and N) doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) materials were synthesized via facile hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as a nitrogen source and 3-aminophenyl boronic acid (3APBA) as a boron source. The synthesis parameters were optimized and optimal synthesis temperature and reaction time were 260 °C and 2 h, respectively. The chemical structures were characterized using various techniques. The experimental results showed that B,N co-doped CQDs had the strongest fluorescence intensity when the weight ratio of 3APBA:o-PD was 3:1 and showed a phenomenal quantum yield of 59% with high selectivity for Fe3+ and Hg2+ ions, with quenching constants 1.25 × 103 M–1 and 8.3 × 103 M–1, respectively. The high fluorescence quenching was attributed to the photoinduced electron transfer phenomenon, making it suitable for fluorescent sensing materials. Cytotoxicity data revealed synthesized materials are safe for biomedical applications within a 1–10 µg/mL concentration.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently carbon-based materials have wide applications in various sectors. In contemporary times, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as the predominant carbon nanomaterials, alongside graphene and carbon nanotubes. These nanoparticles possess conductive properties attributed to edge effects and quantum confinement and exhibit a size range spanning from 1 to 10 nm. The luminous characteristics of this substance have garnered significant attention. [1]. CQDs are widely used in various disciplines including drug delivery, sensors, ion detection, and cell imaging owing to their low cost, easy fabrication, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [2-4]. The autofluorescence shown by organisms mostly occurs within the wavelength range of 400–600 nm. However, in the context of biomedicine and optoelectronic components, it is observed that most CQDs tend to produce fluorescence in the blue and green regions (with shorter wavelengths) upon exposure to UV light [5]. Moreover, the use of CQDs in biomedical and optoelectronic components is restricted [6], due to low fluorescence yield and poor surface structure [7]. Thus, it is speculated that heteroatom doping or surface passivation will be useful in modifying the characteristics of CQDs [8]. Typically, the organic solvents or catalysts required for the surface passivation procedure, which are hazardous and complex, as a result its applicability hindered for environment and living things [9,10]. Meanwhile, the particle sizes of the metals and carbon atoms are significantly different for traditional metal atom blending to be successful. When the diameters of the metal atoms are identical with carbon atom, the aforesaid difficulties do not arise, leading to a homogenous mixture which could alter the electronic structure of CQDs effectively.

Therefore, the ideal approach is thus to modify via co-doping heteroatoms (i.e., N, B, S, and/or P) to enhance optical and electrical characteristics including chemical composition and electronic structure [11,12]. The primary prerequisite for adopting CQDs in almost all applications is a high quantum yield (QY). Recently studies proved that S-doped CQDs offer a wide range of potential applications because S atom can alter the bond length and bond angle in the CQDs, which improves the CQDs hydrophilicity, conductivity, and porous structure [13-15]. However, S-doped carbon nanomaterials are less stable in terms of thermal stability and breakdown during electrochemical operations because C-S bonds are weaker than CC bonds [16]. Meanwhile, there are still several drawbacks to phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped CQDs, i.e., for acidic oxidation, including severe environments and a lengthy procedure to remove excessive acid [17,18]. Only nitrogen atoms can be easily blended into organic carbon compounds and frequently integrated to produce CQDs due to their comparable sizes [19]. However, N-doped CQDs (NCQDs) are highly unstable and prone to degradation in acidic and basic environments [20], although they can offer more active sites [21]. Boron is a P-type semiconductor with an electron-deficient atom which displays various characteristics when blended into CQDs. The literature suggests that boron doping alone can increase biological absorption, stability, and quantum yield. Boron doping in CQDs showed blueshift and enhanced fluorescence, which is more favorable parameters for the real applications. Moreover, boron doped CQDs (BCQDs) shows potential use in fluorescence probe applications including hydrogen peroxide [22], and glucose [23] detection. Shan et al. [22] used a hydrothermal method to create a novel, high QY solid-state fluorescent BCQDs. Boron doping resulted in electronic defects, which caused charge transfer between CQDs and prevented agglomeration-induced extraction quenching [22]. Moreover, co-doping with nitrogen (graphitic N) can decrease the nonradiative recombination and hydrogen bonds created by an abundance of B-OH groups, which gives BCQDs the same fluorescence intensity in solid form as it does when disperse in water [14]. Wei et al. synthesized B,N co-doped CQDs by a rapid microwave assisted hydrothermal method for cellular imaging with a low QY (13.79%) [24]. Wang et al. prepared a B,N co-doped CQDs with 59.01% quantum yield for Cr(Ⅵ) detection [25]. Incorporating the aforementioned heteroatoms into CQDs allows for the achievement of tunable fluorescent quantum dots and improved quantum yield, expanding its applications in biomedical, optoelectronics, sensors, and fluorescence probes while preserving the good water solubility, low toxicity, and good chemical stability [24-29]. However, all of the above studies have a low QY, which hinder its practical applications. The low quantum yield depends upon different factors such as synthesis method, synthesis parameter, and type of precursor [30]. Therefore, this study adopts all the above factors to synthesize the B,N co-doped CQDs.

Nevertheless, several precursors have been used to develop high-performance of CQDs, including the cross-linking and polymerization of biomass, polymers, and organic molecules [31-35]. o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) is one of the most significant among them because of its self-doping (N doping) behavior. The conjugated structure of o-PD molecules enables them to function as sources of both nitrogen and carbon, which are crucial elements of PL centers [36]. It also offers an amino functional group that improves the photostabilities of fluorescent CQDs [37]. Meanwhile, 3-aminophenylboronic acid (3APBA) can have three different functions in the synthesis of N, B co-doped CQDs. To increase quantum yield, the amino group is a good source of N-doping, and the benzene ring structure serves as a great source of carbon while the boronic acid group can be a direct source of boron [38]. High pressure and temperature would cause the structure of phenylboronic acid to disintegrate, forming carbon dots [39]. The boric acid (BA) group of 3APBA can be substituted with metal ions owing to their special transmetalation activity [40].

The hydrothermal method is a type of solvothermal method which involves chemical reactions in a closed container at high temperature and high pressure [41]. Since the reaction in the solution produces CQDs with good dispersion, it is frequently used to synthesize or blend CQDs [42,43]. The synthesis method is not only easy and efficient, but also significantly increases the number of defects. Surface defects can be applied to produce surface modification effects in addition to altering quantum yield and fluorescence properties [44]. Numerous publications have described the use of food or agricultural wastes, such as milk [45], sugarcane bagasse [46], and corn stalks [47] as precursor to the hydrothermal method of synthesizing CQDs. The main advantages of the hydrothermal approach are its moderate reaction conditions, ability to add different precursors simultaneously for the reaction, and environmental friendliness [41,48].

Herein, we synthesized B,N co-doped CQDs via facile hydrothermal method using o-PD and 3APBA as nitrogen and boron source, respectively. Furthermore, the synthesis parameters were optimized. The optical and physiochemical properties were evaluated including cationic detection along with paraquat and H2O2 in aqueous medium. Additionally, cytotoxicity behavior of CQDs were evaluated against 3T3 fibroblast cells using 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-Diphenyltetrazolium Bromide (MMT) assay. The fluorescence stability of B,N co-doped CQD was analyzed using different pH environment.

o-PD (98%), Iron (II) chloride (95% Cobalt (II) chloride (99%), and Magnesium chloride (99%) were purchased from Alfa Aesar, UK. 3APBA (98%), ethyl alcohol (95%), acetone (95%) and Rhodamine B (95%) were obtained from Jingming Chemical, Taiwan. Iron (III) chloride (95%) and Mercury (II) chloride (99.5%) were acquired from Acros UK. Potassium chloride (99%) was received from SHOWA, Japan. Other reagents and chemicals were received and used as such.

The nitrogen-doped CQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. o-PD (1.5 g) was dissolved in deionized water (50 mL) and then stirred using a magnetic stirrer to obtain a homogenous solution. Then the suspension was transferred into a 75 mL autoclave with a quartz cup. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at different temperatures as shown in Table S1, and naturally cooled down to the ambient temperature. Consequently, the dialysis was carried out for 24 h using a 500–1000 Da dialysis membrane, followed by filtration by 0.02 μm PVDF filter and freeze dried at – 40 ℃ for 72 h. To find out the optimal parameter, the reaction was performed at different temperatures for a fixed time. After screening out the best temperature (260 °C) and the reaction time was adjusted (Table S2). The fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) was used to detect the optimized parameters.

B,N co-doped CQDs were fabricated via hydrothermal method using o-PD and 3APBA as percussors. The different proportions of o-PD and 3APBA were evenly mixed in 50 mL of deionized water, and then stirred using a magnetic stirrer for 20 min. Then the solution was placed the quartz cup autoclave (75 mL). The hydrothermal synthesis process was carried out at 260 °C for 2 h (optimum condition) and then naturally cooled down to ambient temperature. Consequently, dialysis was carried out using a 500–1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, followed by filtration with a 0.02 μm PVDF filter, and freeze dried at – 40 ℃ for 72 h. For comparison, various ratios of the precursors were taken. The detailed sample coding and ratios of o-PD and 3APBA were given in Table S3, and the fluorescence spectra were used to detect the optimal precursor ratio. Fig. 1 briefly explains the synthesis schematic of B,N co-doped CQDs (BNCQDs).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to examine the CQDs microstructural morphology (HR-TEM; FEI Talos F200s). Using a Fison VG ESCA210 spectrometer with Mg‒Kα radiation, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to investigate the chemical composition of the materials. For the XPS analysis first NCQDs and BNCQDs solutions were deposited on Si substrates and allowed to dry at room temperature. The spectra (C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s) were deconvoluted using a symmetric Gaussian function and the non-linear least squares fitting approach, all spectra were. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer, Llantrisant) analysis was done to investigate the surface functional groups. FTIR spectra were acquired from 4000 to 600 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 16 scans. The thermal stability of samples was evaluated using a Thermogravimetric analyzer (Perkin Elmer Pyris 1, TGA) instrument in nitrogen atmosphere. During analysis, samples were heated at a rate of 10 °C per minute from 100 to 800 °C.

The absorbance spectra of the suspensions were examined using an ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis; JASCO 670, Japan) spectrometer. Each suspension was prepared with distilled water (10 ppm) in the quartz cells, and a scan rate of 60 nm/min was employed. To examine the fluorescence (PL) emissions spectra of suspensions, a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-7000 FLS920P) was used. Using the following equation, the samples' QY was calculated in relation to Rhodamine B (QY: 95% at 552 nm excitation).
(1)
Q Y
=
Q
Y
r e f e r e n c e
×
[
(
P L
a r e a
O D
)
s a m p l e
(
P L
a r e a
O D
)
r e f e r e n c e
]
×
[
η 2
s a m p l e
η 2
r e f e r e n c e
]
Where, the sample and reference are denoted, respectively, by the subscripts. Here, ‘η’ represents the solvent's reflective index, and PL and OD stand for the absorbance value and fluorescence area, respectively.

In this study, the NCQDs and BNCQDs were used as probing material. This study employed cations such as Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Cu+ as targeting ions along with fructose, glucose, nitrite, xylose, galactose, lactose, urea, panadol, paraquat and H2O2. Herein the ionic concentration was set at 10–2 M for the ionic detection in liquid phase while the concentration of fructose, glucose, nitrite, xylose, galactose, lactose, urea, panadol, paraquat and H2O2 was kept at 100 ppm. For accuracy, the detection measurements were carried out in triplicate. The relative PL intensity,
F /
F 0
versus ionic concentration was found to have a linear function as the calibration curve. Herein, F 0 and F were the PL intensities of CQD suspensions in the absence and presence of different concentrations of metal ions, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of samples was measured using time-resolved photoluminescence measurements with multifunctional spectrophotometer fitted with a pulsed diode laser. The fluorescence lifetime is calculated by fitting the attenuation of fluorescence intensity as a single exponential function. Moreover, the effect of pH on the PL intensity was observed over a wide range of pH (1–13). The pH was modulated by adding 1 M of HCl or NaOH.

The cytotoxicity behavior of synthesized samples (NCQDs and BNCQD-5) was evaluated using MMT assay against 3T3 fibroblast cell line, as per the previous study [49]. Optical density was measured at the fixed wavelength of 550 nm by using an Epoch2 microplate reader (BioTek) [50]. Cell viability was calculated by using the following expression:
(2)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
Absorbance
of
treated
cells
Absorbance
of
control
cells
× 100
The reported result was calculated as the average of three replications and presented as a percentage of the control sample value.

The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs is influenced by various experimental conditions like reaction temperature, duration, and solvent [51]. The best fluorescence intensity is achieved by controlling reaction duration and temperature. The experiments were conducted at different temperatures, with a 20 °C temperature difference, while maintaining a constant reaction duration of 2 h. After a series of purification steps, freeze-drying and centrifugation, a 100-ppm concentration was prepared to detect the fluorescence intensity at each temperature as shown in Fig. S1. The results showed that emission band is significantly improved at 420 nm wavelength, when the excitation band gradually redshifts from 300 nm to 480 nm. At 420 nm, the emission reaches a maximum and then gradually decreases (Table S1). After sorting the maximum emission at each temperature, it is finally concluded that the NCQDs synthesized at 260 °C has the highest fluorescence intensity. Experiments with varying reaction durations are carried out using the prior optimal condition (reaction temperature of 260 °C) and characterized by fluorescence spectrometer. The fluorescence intensity exhibits a decreasing trend as the reaction time increases, as shown in Fig. S2. Combining with the earlier findings, as illustrated in Fig. 2a and b, it is determined that reaction at 260 °C for 2 h is the ideal reaction condition for NCQDs. The produced NCQDs exhibit the best emission intensity at 600 nm, with a Stokes shift of 180 nm, and an ideal excitation wavelength of 420 nm (Table S2).

Similarly, 3APBA was added to the o-PD precursor solution under the best reaction conditions to modify the NCQDs. The precursors ratio affects the size, fluorescence, and QY of quantum dots, accordingly various boron concentrations were combined. The samples detail was given in Table S3. Following measurement, it was determined that the emission wavelength is gradually increasing, as the excitation wavelength red shifts from 380 nm to 480 nm. According to Fig. S3, the results demonstrate that BNCQD-5 has a maximum emission wavelength of 540 nm and a Stokes shift of 100 nm when excited at 440 nm. As seen in Fig. 2c, the optimal fluorescence intensity was found at a weight ratio of 3APBA:o-PD at 3:1, comparing the highest intensities from BNCQD-1 to BNCQD-5.

The DLS result (Fig. S4) indicates that the NCQDs and BNCQDs sizes are <10 nm. The average size of NCQDs is 3 nm (Fig. S4a), but the average size of BNCQD-5 is 9 nm (Fig. S4b). The above data indicates that quantum dot size tends to enlarge following boron doping. Further, the size and morphology were determined from TEM analysis. The TEM images (Fig. 3a and c) show that both the NCQDs and BNCQDs form a quasi-spherical shape (i.e., circular and elliptical) [52]. The borders of the particles are indicated here by the dashed red curves. Considering the lattice fringes that formed on both NCQDs and BNCQDs nanoparticles, excellent crystallinity can be seen in every sample. A lattice distance of approximately 0.24 nm (100) is measured for NCQDs (Fig. 3b ), while two lattice fringes of 0.21 and 0.24 nm are observed for (100) plane for BNCQDs (Fig. 3d). The reduction of the interplanar spacing of carbon resulting from B doping may be the cause of the appearance of the 0.21 nm lattice spacing in BNCQDs [53]. As per TEM analysis, the CQDs range from 5 to 10 nm (Fig. S5).

XPS measurements of BNCQDs and NCQDs were performed to get insight into the surface chemical composition. Fig. 4a displays the full scan XPS spectrum. BNCQD-5 contains an additional B peak near 200 eV in comparison to NCQDs. Carbon (66.67%), nitrogen (5.44%), and oxygen (27.89%) make up the majority of NCQDs. BNCQD-5 is primarily composed of 45.72% carbon, 8.72% nitrogen, 31.3% oxygen, and 14.54% boron (Table S4). The primary peaks of BNCQD-5 are at 192 eV, 286 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV, whereas the main peaks of CQDs are at 286 eV, 401 eV, and 533 eV for carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively. BNCQD-5 exhibits a higher degree of oxidation and acylation than NCQDs, resulting in superior hydrophilicity and fluorescence [51]. This is indicated by the higher O/C and a N/C ratio of BNCQD-5 than NCQDs.

From Fig. 4a, it is evident that the boron signal at 200 eV increases noticeably when the 3APBA content increases in the BNCQDs. Table S5 provides more clarity on the variations to the overall element content. As can be observed, there is a declining trend in the nitrogen content, which is caused primarily by the precursor's amino group decrease. Doping N-type (N) and P-type (B) heteroatoms could change the luminescence effect by increasing the amount of surface holes and electrons, respectively. According to Table S5, the heteroatom blending amounts for BNCQD-1 (B1) and BNCQD-2 (B2) are 18.7% and 19.8%, respectively. These amounts are significantly smaller than those for BNCQD-3 (B3), BNCQD-4 (B4), and BNCQD-5 (B5), which are 23.51%, 23.57%, and 23.26%, respectively. As a result, the quantum yield of the former two samples (B1 and B2) is lower than that of the latter.

Nitrogen content typically has a large influence on fluorescence intensity. B3 has the lowest yield out of the last three, although having the highest nitrogen content. Table S5 suggests that the amino group, instead of the boric acid, makes up the majority of the 3APBA blending into the quantum dots, therefore, O/C ratio weakens in B3. Reduced hydrophilicity allows for the possibility of aggregation in water, which influences quantum yield and fluorescence emission intensity. In comparison to B4, B5 contains a larger proportion of aromatic N in the aromatic ring in addition to having a higher total nitrogen content. Fluorescence emission will be reduced by the loss of PL energy, and structural stiffness and vibration will be improved by an increase in the amount of aromatic N. Furthermore, B4 and B5 have aromatic N/C contents of 9.45% and 13.87%, respectively. Better n-π* transition and resonance, as well as more good fluorescence characteristics, are associated with higher proportions of aromatic N.

Figs. S6a and 4b displays the high-resolution spectra of C1s from NCQDs and BNCQD-5, respectively. The CO peak of BNCQD-5 is more prominent, which is also consistent with the result of a higher oxygen atom content in the full spectrum. Different carbon bonds can be observed, which can be mainly divided into CC/CC bonds at 284.5 eV, CN bonds at 285.6 eV, COH bonds at 286.3 eV, and CO bonds at 287.2 eV [52]. Figs. S6b and 4c display the high-resolution O1s spectra of NCQDs and BNCQD-5, respectively, revealing presence of several oxygen functional groups. The two primary bonds of CQDs are CO bonds at 532 eV and CO bonds at 534 eV [51]. Since 3APBA is utilized as the boron source in BNCQD-5, there is also an extra signal of the BO bond at 533 eV in addition to the two main bonds stated above. The N1s high-resolution spectra of BNCQD-5 and NCQDs are displayed in Figs. 4d and S6c respectively. Several nitrogen functional groups can be seen at 399.4 eV (pyrrole/pyridinic), 400.7 eV (quaternary), and 402.1 eV (OCN) in both NCQDs and BNCQD-5 which indicates that the nitrogen was successfully absorbed into the crystal lattice by the hydrothermal synthesis process. [20]. Furthermore, the B1s high-resolution spectrum of BNCQD-5 is displayed in Fig. 4e. The main distinct peaks are BCO2 at 192.4 eV, B-N bonding at 193.4 eV, and BO bond at 194.2 eV [6,53]. The O1s and B1s split peak spectra, as well as the entire XPS spectrum, display the signals of boron atoms which confirm the successful integration of B atom into the NCQDs.

The presence of functional groups on the surface both before and after boron doping is confirmed by the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 4f which displays the peaks from 3200 cm–1 to 3600 cm–1 that can be divided into NH, -OH, and CH bonds [20]. The peaks at 1780 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 are caused by the vibrations of carbon structures such as CO and CN [6]. Peaks ranging from 1150 cm–1 to 1300 cm–1 may be indicative of the extension of bonds, such as CO and COOH, suggesting the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups in the sample [20]. Three separate peaks on BNCQD-5, located at 1134 cm–1, 1327 cm–1 and 1449 cm–1, correspond to surface bonds such B-OH, B-O, and B-N [6]. Hence, it may be concluded that boron atoms have been well integrated into the quantum dot surface. FTIR results are in agreement with earlier XPS findings as well, providing more evidence that boron atoms have been integrated into quantum dots with success. The TGA spectrum, as displayed in Fig. S7, demonstrates that the thermal stability of quantum dots rises with the amount of B atom, with BNCQD-5 clearly having the highest thermal stability.

Compared to NCQD (Fig. 5a ), the optimal excitation wavelength of BNCQD-5 changed from 420 nm to 440 nm, but the Stokes shift was shortened from 180 nm to 100 nm, resulting in a small blue shift in the emission wavelength (from 600 nm to about 540 nm) (Fig. 5b). BNCQD-5 absorption peak around 435 nm, possibly due to an increase in nitrogen incorporation content (∼ 60%), causing changes in the size and structure of defects and changes in the energy gap. In addition, the sample was also placed under ultraviolet light and visible light to observe the actual color. From this, the luminescence changes from the original orange to yellow-green fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 5c.

The UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 5d shows the presence of π-π* and n-π* signals at 230 nm and 300 nm, respectively, in NCQDs. These signals could be attributed to CC and CO/CN transitions in NCQDs [51]. Compared to NCQDs, BNCQD-5 has a large red shift in the π-π* and n-π* signals, and the band gap of BNCQD-5 is relatively smaller. One possible explanation for the observed shift in the band gap is doping, as the addition of boron atoms increases the amount of hole NCQDs, increasing the possibility of electron hole extraction while using less energy. Additionally, the energy band gap of BNCQD-5 is somewhat smaller, as shown by the Tauc's plot in Fig. 5e and f. In conclusion, the optimal excitation wavelength of CQDs changes with boron doping which causes a small blue shift and changes the energy band gap.

To apply the synthesized quantum dots for the metal ion detection, various metal ions (Co2+, Cu+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, H2O2, K+, and Fe2+) were added to NCQDs and BNCQD-5. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . From Fig. 6a, it can be seen that after adding Fe3+ and Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity drops by more than half, while for the remaining ions, fluorescence maintains a certain level of intensity. From the graph of fluorescence intensity ratio (F0: the original fluorescence intensity, F: the fluorescence intensity after adding ions) in Fig. 6b, it can be clearly seen that after adding the above two ions into NCQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity reduced to 25% and 36% respectively. Furthermore, the NCQDs solution with different metal ions added was photographed under ultraviolet and visible light. In the digital photo (Fig. 6c), it can be seen with the naked eye that there is an obvious extraction quenching phenomenon after the addition of iron and mercury ions. The same experiment was carried out on BNCQD-5. From Fig. 6d, although the fluorescence fluctuates greatly after adding various ions, the degrees of quenching of Fe3+ and Hg2+ are distinct, and the fluorescence intensities are reduced to <20%, and from the fluorescence intensity ratio, the fluorescence intensity is left at 7% and 8% respectively (Fig. 6e). The BNCQD-5 solution with different metal ions is also photographed under ultraviolet and visible light (Fig. 6f). It can be seen that for both iron and mercury ions, the degree of quenching is remarkable, indicating that BNCQD-5 has great potential in developing fluorescent probes that can be recognized by the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction quenching mechanism and stability experiment
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigations were carried out to determine the feasibility of using the synthesized quantum dots in fluorescent probes and to determine the quenching mechanism. Fig. 8 displays the UV absorption spectra of NCQDs and BNCQD-5 with Fe3+ and Hg2+. It can be observed that, for both NCQDs and BNCQD-5 (Fig. 8a and c), the absorption peak does not significantly change upon the addition of Fe3+, and that the majority of the Fe3+ absorption peak overlaps with both of the two CQDs’ excitation wavelengths. Thus, an internal filtering effect (IFE) is proposed as the extraction quenching mechanism for Fe3+[60]. The absorption peak at 450 nm of NCQDs and BNCQD-5 shifts upon the addition of Hg2+(Fig. 8b and d). Consequently, there is probability that Hg2+ will form new compounds through chelation with quantum dots. When coupled with Ksv, due to the chelation reaction, the Hg2+has a smaller extraction quenching constant and a slower reaction rate, whereas the Fe3+has a bigger Ksv because it has faster reaction time. In the low concentration range, Ksv >102 shows a strong linear correlation (static), even though it often indicates dynamic extraction quenching. This prompts the possibility that the extraction quenching mechanism could be caused by a mix of these two mechanisms. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime was measured to further investigate the quenching mechanism. From Fig. S9, it can be observed that the average fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs (5.3 ns) was improved after the integration of Boron atom, which signifies the improvement of optical properties [61]. Also, the average fluorescence lifetime of BNCQD-5 (6 ns) decreased with the addition of Fe3+ (3.48 ns) and Hg2+ (3.57 ns). Similar trends can be observed in previous literature [62,54]. This change in the fluorescence lifetime clearly indicates a typical dynamic quenching. The overall quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 9 .

The stability of fluorescence was examined in various pH environments and after the incorporation of biological molecules like fructose and glucose. Fig. 10 displays the PL intensity in different pH. These CQDs can be used to detect metal ions because they have certain functional groups on their surface. At a lower pH, the fluorescence intensity of BNCQD-5 was comparatively low, suggesting the presence of certain acidic groups on their surface. On the other hand, fluorescence intensity of BNCQD-5 stayed relatively constant in the pH 4–10 solution indicating that the synthesized quantum dots are appropriate for the sensing application in majority of environments [63]. When the pH value exceeded 10, the fluorescence intensity again dropped. The BNCQD-5′s declining fluorescence intensity behavior in highly acidic and alkaline media may be linked to the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups, which disrupts the surface charge. While under basic conditions, deprotonation eliminates H-bonding and can result in irregular energy levels that lower the fluorescence of BNCQD-5. Furthermore, H+ can create surface imperfections on BNCQD-5 by rupturing the passivated OH-shell, which causes decrement in PL intensity [64]. Lastly, some common substances found in organisms or food (glucose, fructose, nitrite, xylose, galactose, lactose, urea, Panadol) were used for selective experiments to determine the viability of using the synthesized CQDs for detection. The results (Fig. S10) showed that the fluorescence intensity remained essentially unchanged, indicating that it should be suitable for the various environments mentioned above.

Cytotoxicity should be investigated prior to the application of biomaterials to avoid the occurrence of adverse impacts on the living organism. Accordingly, cytotoxicity behavior of NCQDs and BNCQD-5 samples was evaluated against 3T3 fibroblast cells using MMT assay. The results are presented in Fig. 11 . The results revealed that both samples are highly compatible within 1–10 µg/mL concentration. However, NCQDs sample cell viability decreased (<80%) in high concentration (25 µg/mL). The above results proved that BNCQD-5 sample has better potential in the field of biomedical applications. Overall, The above results suggested that NCQDs and BNCQD-5 sample are more suitable for biomedical in the 1–10 µg/mL concentration range.

The work discusses the screening method for synthesizing nitrogen functionalized quantum dots using o-PD precursors as nitrogen and carbon sources and B,N co-doped-CQDs using 3APBA as a boron source. The optimal reaction conditions were used to synthesize boron-containing BNCQDs. The XPS and FTIR results suggest successful boron doping. The UV-visible and fluorescence spectrums show that the mixed BNCQDs produces a blue shift in UV light, possibly due to strong electron-attracting ability. The fluorescence intensity and quantum yield increased from 24% (NCQDs) to 59% (BNCQD-5). The size of the synthesized NCQDs particle is about 3 nm, while the BNCQD-5 size is about 9 nm. Both NCQDs and BNCQD-5 have excellent detection abilities for Fe3+ and Hg2+. NCQDs showed a strong linear correlation within the concentration range of 0–200 µL for Fe3+ and 20–200 µL for Hg2+, while BNCQDs showed a strong linear correlation within the concentration range of 0–200 µL for Fe3+ and 20–200 µL for Hg2+ respectively. BNCQDs has good fluorescence stability at pH values between 4–10. This study suggests that B,N co-doped CQDs can be a potential candidate in fluorescence probe for the detection of heavy and toxic ions in aqueous media.

Rashmiranjan Patra: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Pradeep Kumar Panda: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chang-Yuan Hung: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Pranjyan Dash: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Po-Chih Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chien-Te Hsieh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
